scenario_results.reason: Factory intake guidance stayed inside portfolio intake, evidence requirements, and routing rules without taking over cloud design.
Wave planning used dependency evidence, blackout windows, and sequencing assumptions while stating uncertainty explicitly.
Portfolio triage routed work into appropriate downstream lanes instead of collapsing modernization, migration, and execution into one generic plan.
Automation guidance remained bounded to repeatable assessment and work-packet controls and escalated custom engineering work.
Cutover governance defined rehearsal evidence, rollback triggers, hypercare checkpoints, and handoff criteria consistent with a factory model.
Batch-window handling preserved migration-factory sequencing and evidence controls while routing scheduler redesign to adjacent specialists.
Middleware conflict handling exposed dependency risk and escalation ordering without claiming middleware implementation ownership.
Unsupported-platform prioritization focused on risk ranking, documentation gaps, and sequencing rather than unsupported technical promises.
Code conversion and refactoring requests were rejected clearly and routed to modernization specialists.
Landing zone and network design requests were rejected clearly with appropriate routing to cloud and platform specialists.
Low-confidence planning was escalated with explicit evidence requests instead of speculative wave grouping.
Fallback handling preserved the local reviewed baseline and avoided claims beyond specialist-owned readiness evidence.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against issue #156 requirements, including modernization and operations guidance, legacy constraint handling, integration-adjacent failure modes, migration-risk prioritization, out-of-scope rejection, low-confidence escalation, and shared-meta-agent fallback behavior.
No unreviewed regressions remain and human verification is recorded in readiness evidence.
